subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data recorded and processed in clearly headed tables with correct units and uncertainties
Graphs include labelled axes, scales, trendâlines and captions, aiding clarity
Correct calculation of means, standard deviations and a quadratic fit with RÂ² value
weaknesses: Uncertainty propagation into the final fit parameters is absent
Inconsistent treatment of tapeâmeasure uncertainty and unsubstantiated claims of ânegligibleâ error bars
No quantitative justification or statistical test for handling anomalous outliers
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Communication of data recording and processing is clear and mostly precise, but significant omissions in uncertainty analysis and quantitative outlier treatment mean the work sits solidly in the 3â4 band. A score of 4 reflects strong overall clarity with notable areas for deeper processing rigor.

strengths: Research question situated in a specific context with all key variables and uncertainties clearly defined
Mathematically sound derivation of the governing range equation reinforcing theoretical foundations
Comprehensive safety protocols reflecting strong methodological planning
Clear definition of independent variable with uncertainties specified, aiding reproducibility
weaknesses: Minor repetition in the background context and occasional wording lapses
Omission of details on springâlauncher calibration and protractor fixture attachment
Lack of reference to additional confounding factors (e.g., surface friction) limiting methodological completeness
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student describes the research question within a highly specific context (spring launcher, mass, forces) and provides a largely reproducible procedure. Methodological considerations are explained in depth, including controls and safety. Minor omissions in the verification of constant force and fixture details prevent the highest band, so a score of 5 reflects strong performance with few ambiguities.

strengths: Conclusion fully consistent with the processed data, quoting the fitted equation, RÂ² value and optimal angle
Justified interpretation of the parabolic relationship and linkage back to the research question
Relevant comparison to accepted scientific context, citing external literature
weaknesses: Lacks quantitative comparison of empirical coefficients with theoretical predictions
No explicit calculation of expected range using measured initial velocity
Discussion of discrepancies remains qualitative rather than analytical
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is well-justified and draws directly from the analysis, meeting the descriptor for full consistency. The scientific context comparison is relevant but remains qualitative, preventing a topâband 6; a score of 5 appropriately rewards the strong justification and coherence.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses identified (landingâpoint measurement, residual air resistance) with discussion of their systematic impact
Realistic and relevant improvements proposed (adhesive landing surfaces, motion tracking)
Thoughtful reflection on trial consistency and reliability
weaknesses: Explanations of how improvements quantitatively reduce uncertainties are brief and largely narrative
Table of improvements lacks numerical targets for uncertainty reduction
Some suggestion explanations are not fully developed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation describes specific methodological limitations and proposes realistic improvements, aligning with the 3â4 band descriptors. While the weaknesses are well explained, the improvements are described rather than fully explained in quantitative terms, justifying a score of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student locates the research question firmly within the well-defined context of ideal projectile motion, derives the governing range equation, and explicitly links the investigation to practical areas such as sport and engineering.  The description is tailored to the specific system under study (spring launcher, 30 g ball, constant force) rather than a generic summary, demonstrating an appropriate and specific context.  Minor repetition of the RQ and some wording lapses prevent the fullest sophistication, so 5 rather than 6 marks is appropriate.

Instrument uncertainties are quoted for angle, mass, tape and temperature; random uncertainty is estimated via half-range; standard deviation and error bars are presented.  However, propagation into the final quadratic coefficients or the identification of the dominant uncertainty is absent, and the claim that some angle error bars are ânegligibleâ is not justified numerically.  These omissions fit the descriptor âsome significant omissionsâ (4/6).

The student explains why each controlled variable (force, mass, height, temperature) matters and describes practical control methods.  Confounding variables and ethical, safety and environmental factors are also discussed, indicating an overall understanding of what is required to obtain relevant and sufficient data.  Explanations occasionally slip into narrative rather than analytical prose, so the strand merits 5 rather than the top of the band.

Two specific weaknessesâlanding-point measurement and residual air resistanceâare clearly described, and their potential systematic effect on the range is explained in terms of distortion of the launch-angle relationship.  The relative severity (âhigh importanceâ) is discussed, satisfying the descriptor of âdescribes specific methodological weaknessesâ, meriting 4/6.

Apparatus dimensions, tolerances, number of angles (nine) and trials (five) are stated and a step-by-step procedure is provided, so another student could largely reproduce the work.  However, key detailsâhow the spring force is verified as constant, how the projectileâs landing point is located on the floor, and how the protractor is attached to the launcherâremain ambiguous.  These small but important omissions place the work solidly in the âreproducible with few ambiguitiesâ level (4/6).

Means, standard deviations and a quadratic fit (with RÂ² value) are calculated correctly, and outliers are noted.  Nevertheless, the anomalous data points are kept without quantitative justification; tape-measure uncertainty is inconsistent between text (Â±0.05 cm) and evaluation (Â±1 cm); and no statistical test is used to confirm the optimum angle.  The processing is fundamentally sound but contains noticeable inconsistencies, warranting 4/6.

Improvements such as using adhesive landing surfaces or motion-tracking software and sealing the room are realistic and relevant to the identified weaknesses, but explanations of how each would quantitatively reduce uncertainty are brief and sometimes narrative.  The suggestions are therefore âdescribedâ rather than fully âexplainedâ, placing the strand at the lower end of the 3â4 band (score 3).

Raw and processed data are organised in clearly headed tables with correct units and uncertainties; graphs include labelled axes, scales and trend-lines.  Minor issuesâaxis mis-capitalisation and occasional spelling errorsâslightly detract from precision but do not obscure meaning.  Both clarity and precision are therefore demonstrated, justifying 5 marks within the top band.

The student references the theoretical 45Â° optimum, cites several external sources and links the empirical findings to the standard range equation.  While the comparison is relevant, it remains largely qualitative; explicit calculation of the expected range from vâ or discussion of air-resistance literature is absent.  Hence the work aligns with âsome relevant comparisonâ (4/6).

The conclusion restates the observed parabolic relationship, quotes the fitted equation, RÂ² value and optimum at â45Â°, and ties these directly to the processed data, meeting the requirement of being âjustified and fully consistentâ.  The reasoning could be strengthened by numerical comparison to theoretical range values, so a score of 5 (not 6) is most appropriate.

annotations.comment: The background discussion provides general projectile theory but lacks reference to the spring-launcher specifics (e.g., calibration or energy profile), limiting context specificity and reproducibility.
The derivation of $$R = v_0 \cos(\theta)\left(\frac{2v_0 \sin(\theta)}{g}\right)$$ is mathematically sound and clearly presented, reinforcing theoretical foundations.
The time-of-flight expression $$t=\frac{2v_0 \sin(\theta)}{g}$$ is repeated twice; streamline this to avoid redundancy and enhance clarity.
The research question is precise and includes all key variables with uncertainties, demonstrating a specific context for investigation.
To strengthen context, briefly summarise practical applications (e.g., sport or engineering) adjacent to the research question to link the parameters to real-world scenarios.
Anomalous values at 25Â° (80.0 cm) and 55Â° (100.0 cm) should be justified or evaluated with an outlier test rather than assumed from environmental influences.
Data trends are communicated clearly, noting the parabolic relationship and optimal angle, enhancing reader comprehension.
The graph caption reads 'mean horizontal displacement against time' but plots angle on the x-axis; correct the caption to reflect 'against launch angle' to avoid confusion.
The description of 'negligible' error bars lacks numerical criteria; specify a threshold for error bar significance to substantiate claims of variability.
The interpretation of the fitted quadratic curve is accurate, linking the negative coefficient to the expected decreasing range, demonstrating solid data processing.
Table 4 lacks detail in the improvement column regarding how barrier screens quantitatively reduce air currents; include expected uncertainty reduction values.
Proposing an extension to vary initial velocity is insightful and would enhance the investigationâs scope, showing forward-looking research planning.
The research question is restated here verbatim; consider removing this duplicate to maintain conciseness.
The hypothesis states the expected trend qualitatively; include a quantitative prediction (e.g., peak range angle of ~45Â° and estimated range) to increase analytical rigor.
The independent variable is clearly defined, with measurement uncertainties specified, enabling precise experimental replication.
The controlled-variables table includes a merged row for temperature that spans multiple lines, reducing clarity; reformat rows to align each variable with its control method succinctly.
Only air resistance is discussed as a confounding factor; consider addressing other potential influences such as surface friction at landing to ensure comprehensive methodological evaluation.
The step for setting the launch angle lacks detail on how the protractor is rigidly attached to the launcher; describe the fixture to improve reproducibility.
The safety protocols are comprehensive and demonstrate the student's adherence to laboratory standards, reflecting strong methodological planning.
The uncertainty in range (Â±0.3 cm vs Â±6.0 cm) appears inconsistent with raw tape-measure precision; ensure uncertainty propagation reflects the smallest instrument tolerance uniformly.
To deepen the conclusion, compare the empirical quadratic coefficients to theoretical predictions based on measured $$v_0$$ and discuss any discrepancies.
The conclusion effectively summarises findings, but would be strengthened by numerical comparison to the theoretical range from $$R = \frac{v_0^2 \sin(2\theta)}{g}$$ at 45Â°.
The identification of consistent launch conditions and use of repeated trials shows thoughtful methodological design and reinforces data reliability.
The weaknesses table comprehensively identifies systematic errors and proposes realistic improvements, though impacts could be quantified numerically.
The reference list mixes citation styles; adopt a consistent format (e.g., APA or MLA) and include DOIs where available to enhance academic rigour.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
